message.content:
The salt had long since bleached Elias’s skin to the color of old parchment, and the wind off the North Atlantic had a voice of its own, screaming through the cracks of the stone tower. For thirty years, Elias had been the sole witness to the graveyard of ships that lay just beyond the breakwater. He knew the rhythm of the gales, the taste of the storm, and the silence that followed. He thought he knew everything there was to know about the sea.

Then came the Tuesday of the gray mist.

It wasn’t a storm that brought the bottle, but a calm so unnatural it felt like the ocean was holding its breath. A single object bobbed against the jagged rocks below the lighthouse gallery. Elias, expecting driftwood or a buoy, climbed down the spiral stairs, his knees popping in protest, and waded into the icy surf.

It was a bottle, thick glass, green as deep kelp, sealed with wax that had somehow resisted the decades. Inside was a scroll, tightly wound and preserved in oilcloth.

Elias carried it up to the lantern room, the heart of his isolation. He broke the seal with a trembling hand. The paper inside was not paper at all, but a thin sheet of synthetic material, shimmering faintly under the beam of the rotating light. The handwriting was precise, angular, and unmistakably modern—yet the date scrawled in the corner read *October 14, 2042*.

Elias frowned. He was born in 1950. He was ninety-two years old.

He read the message, and the world tilted on its axis.

*"Elias,"* it began. *"If you are reading this, the loop has closed. Do not trust the calm. The sea is not water; it is a mirror. You have been keeping the
